concepts.name: Portfolio Map
concepts.definition: A strategic tool to visualize and manage today's business models (Exploit) alongside future business models (Explore). Two halves: Explore Portfolio (Expected Return vs. Innovation Risk) and Exploit Portfolio (Return vs. Death & Disruption Risk). Invincible companies manage both simultaneously.

concepts.name: Explore Portfolio
concepts.definition: The collection of innovation projects that search for new business models, value propositions, and growth engines. Mapped on Expected Return vs. Innovation Risk. Purpose: search — turning ideas into validated, scalable business models.

concepts.name: Four Innovation Risks
concepts.definition: Desirability Risk (customers aren't interested), Viability Risk (can't make money), Feasibility Risk (can't build/deliver), Adaptability Risk (external factors unfavorable). Any one can kill a business idea. Testing reduces each risk with evidence.

concepts.name: Design-Test Loop
concepts.definition: Two iterative loops: Business Design (shaping ideas into business models with best-expected-return) and Testing (testing hypotheses to reduce risk). They feed each other: design a business model → test its riskiest assumptions → use learnings to redesign.

concepts.name: Search and Pivot Journey
concepts.definition: The path through the Explore portfolio: Discovery (understand customers/context) → Validation (prove interest and profit indications) → Acceleration (proven model at limited scale). Includes Reality Check (initial trajectory fails) and Change of Direction (new direction after pivot).

concepts.name: Five Innovation Journey Myths
concepts.definition: (1) Finding the perfect idea is most important — reality: constant testing and adaptation matters more. (2) Evidence shows a clear path forward — reality: evidence is messy and contradictory. (3) Business cases predict success — reality: they can't in high uncertainty. (4) More resources = better results — reality: premature scaling kills innovation. (5) Innovation is a linear process — reality: it's iterative loops.

arguments.claim: Invincible companies don't choose between exploring and exploiting — they do both simultaneously, with different management systems for each
arguments.evidence: The Portfolio Map makes this visible: the Explore side requires a management system optimized for learning and iteration (high tolerance for failure, small investments, speed). The Exploit side requires a management system optimized for efficiency and execution (KPIs, scale, risk management). Mixing the two management systems destroys both.

arguments.claim: Most innovation efforts fail not because of bad ideas but because they are managed with Exploit metrics and processes
arguments.evidence: When an Explore project is measured by ROI, payback period, and quarterly milestones, it's being judged by standards that assume low uncertainty. High-uncertainty projects need different metrics: evidence gained per dollar spent, learning velocity, number of validated/invalidated hypotheses.

terminology.definition: How lucrative a business idea could be if successful. Measured by profitability, revenue potential, growth potential, margins — or social/environmental return for mission-driven organizations.
The chance a business idea will fail. High when there's little evidence (just slides); decreases as evidence about desirability/feasibility/viability accumulates.
A spectrum from high-uncertainty exploration (search) to low-uncertainty exploitation (grow/optimize). Business models move from left to right as evidence accumulates and risk decreases.

nuance: The Explore portfolio is NOT R&D — R&D is about technology. Explore is about business model search. Many large companies have great R&D labs but terrible Explore portfolios.
Not every Explore project should survive. The goal of the Explore portfolio is to kill bad ideas cheap and fast, freeing resources for the ones with evidence.
